verbatim_text: And of course, this is a big simplification because there's significant overlap between these eras. But each era was marked by a dominant paradigm. So starting in the 50s and 60s, an AI model was predominantly the symbolic systems, right? We had all these explicit representations of logical rules and structured knowledge. And then in the 80s and 90s, we shifted towards more probabilistic models. So people started getting interested in modeling uncertainty and these very rigorous Bayesian systems. And then finally, we're in our current era of large neural networks, where models are mostly these big pile of weights that learn from lots and lots of data. So we've kind of lost the structure from earlier eras, but we've gained skill ability. And as a result, our models are a lot more general and powerful. But are we done yet? Have we solved intelligence? Well, no, we haven't. And even as our AI system have scaled dramatically, we still get many persistent failures. So, for example, Air Canada's chatbot hallucinated this non-existent bereavement fair for a grieving passenger.
